beats.text: You've solved problems that needed one step AND problems that needed two steps. Now let's think about HOW you knew what to do.

params.description: Three problem questions displayed as text only: 1. How many more dogs are there than cats? 2. How many more dogs are there than cats and birds combined? 3. How many students chose red or blue altogether?

beats.text: Look at these three questions. Two of them need two steps. One needs just one step.
Which question needs just ONE step?

validator.description: Student selected Question 1, which has no combining words

beats.text: Right. Question 1 just compares dogs to cats. No combining first.

validator.description: Student selected Question 2, which is a two-step problem with combining words

params.description: Question 2 highlights briefly to draw attention

beats.text: Question 2 says combined. That means you need to add first, then compare. Which question has no combining words?

validator.description: Student selected Question 3, which is a two-step problem with combining words

params.description: Question 3 highlights briefly to draw attention

beats.text: Question 3 says altogether. That means you combine first. Which question just compares without combining?

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Question 1 highlights while Questions 2 and 3 dim

beats.text: Let me show you. Question 2 says combined. That means add cats and birds first. Question 3 says altogether. That means add red and blue first. Question 1 just compares dogs to cats. No combining. The answer is Question 1.

elements.description: Three problem questions displayed as text. Student has identified Question 1 as the one-step problem.

beats.text: What's the clue that tells you a problem needs two steps?
What's the clue that tells you a problem needs two steps?

validator.description: Student identified combining words as the signal for two-step problems

beats.text: Words like combined, together, and altogether are your signal. When you see them, you know: find that total first, then compare.

validator.description: Student selected the comparison word, not the combining signal

params.description: The word more in Question 1 and Question 2 highlights briefly

beats.text: The word more tells you to compare. But what tells you to combine first? Look for words about putting things together.

validator.description: Student selected an irrelevant feature

beats.text: The size of the numbers does not tell you how many steps. Look at the words. Which words tell you to put categories together?

validator.description: Student noticed multiple categories but missed the linguistic signal

params.description: Questions 2 and 3 highlight briefly

beats.text: Using two categories is not enough. Question 1 uses dogs and cats but needs just one step. Read Questions 2 and 3 carefully. What words tell you to combine?

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: The words combined in Question 2 and altogether in Question 3 highlight

beats.text: Here is how you know. Question 2 says combined. That word tells you to add cats and birds before you compare. Question 3 says altogether. That word tells you to add red and blue before you compare. Words like combined, together, and altogether are your signal. When you see them, you know the problem needs two steps. The answer is Words like combined or altogether.

elements.description: Three problem questions displayed. Student has identified the linguistic pattern that signals two-step problems.

params.description: Sorting workspace appears. Left side shows three draggable problem cards: (1) 'How many more chose pizza than tacos?', (2) 'How many more chose pizza and tacos combined than salad?', (3) 'How many fewer chose burgers and hot dogs together than pizza?' Right side shows two drop zones labeled 'JUST COMPARE' and 'COMBINE FIRST, THEN COMPARE'.

items.text: How many more chose pizza than tacos?
How many more chose pizza and tacos combined than salad?
How many fewer chose burgers and hot dogs together than pizza?

beats.text: Here's what you've been doing. You look at a problem and decide: Do I just compare? Or do I combine first, then compare? Sort these questions.
Match the task to the group.

validator.description: Student sorted all problems correctly: problem 1 to JUST COMPARE, problems 2 and 3 to COMBINE FIRST

beats.text: You sorted them all. Words like 'combined' and 'together' tell you to find a total first.

validator.description: Student sorted incorrectly on first attempt

beats.text: Not quite. Look for words like 'combined' or 'together' in each problem.

validator.description: Student sorted incorrectly on second attempt

params.description: Problem 2 and Problem 3 pulse briefly to draw attention.

beats.text: Look at problems 2 and 3. They both ask you to combine categories first. Problem 1 asks you to compare two categories directly.

validator.description: Student sorted incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System drags problem 1 to JUST COMPARE zone, then drags problems 2 and 3 to COMBINE FIRST zone.

beats.text: Let me show you. Problem 1 says 'pizza than tacos', so we just compare two categories. Problems 2 and 3 both say 'combined' and 'together', so we combine categories first, then compare. The words tell you which strategy to use.

elements.description: Sorting workspace with three problem cards draggable to two zones: JUST COMPARE and COMBINE FIRST, THEN COMPARE. Drag-to-sort tool active.

params.description: Sorting area fades. Problem cards are no longer draggable.

beats.text: What question do you ask yourself to decide?
Select the key question.

validator.description: Student selected option B, the correct key question

beats.text: Right. That's the key question. The answer tells you whether it's one step or two.

validator.description: Student selected option A: biggest number focus rather than keyword identification

params.description: Option B ('Does it say combined or together?') pulses briefly.

beats.text: The biggest number doesn't tell you the strategy. The words in the problem do. Look for 'combined' or 'together'.

validator.description: Student selected option C: counting categories rather than identifying signal words

params.description: Option B ('Does it say combined or together?') pulses briefly.

beats.text: Counting categories doesn't tell you the plan. The specific words do. When you see 'combined' or 'together', you know to combine first.

validator.description: Student selected option D: operation focus rather than strategic planning question

params.description: Option B ('Does it say combined or together?') pulses briefly.

beats.text: That comes after you decide your strategy. The first question is: Does the problem say 'combined' or 'together'? That tells you if you need to combine categories before comparing.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Option B ('Does it say combined or together?') highlights and remains selected.

beats.text: The key question is: Does it say 'combined' or 'together'? When you see those words, you know you need to combine categories first before comparing. That's the strategic question that tells you your approach.

elements.description: Sorting workspace inactive. Multiple choice options visible: 'What's the biggest number?', 'Does it say 'combined' or 'together'?', 'How many categories are there?', 'Should I add or subtract?'

beats.text: This is strategic thinking. You're not just doing math. You're planning your approach based on what the problem says.

elements.description: Sorting workspace remains on screen, interaction complete.

params.description: Three real-world scenario questions displayed: (1) Who has more stickers—just Maya, or Ben and Carlos put together? (2) Which team scored more—the Red team alone, or the Blue and Green teams combined? (3) Did I spend more on only snacks, or on drinks and dessert altogether?

beats.text: This kind of thinking shows up all the time, not just in math problems. What would you need to do to answer all of these?
What would you need to DO to answer all of these?
options: Combine some things first, then compare

condition.selected: Combine some things first, then compare

validator.description: Student selected option B, combine some things first then compare, correct

beats.text: Right. Combine first, then compare. That pattern shows up everywhere.

validator.description: Student selected 'Count everything': doesn't recognize the need to combine before comparing

params.description: Second scenario highlights: Which team scored more—the Red team alone, or the Blue and Green teams combined?

beats.text: Look at the second question. Red team is alone. Blue and Green need to be put together first. What do we do before we compare?

validator.description: Student selected 'Find the biggest number': focused on individual values rather than the combining pattern

params.description: First scenario highlights: Who has more stickers—just Maya, or Ben and Carlos put together?

beats.text: The first question compares Maya alone to Ben and Carlos put together. We can't just find the biggest number. What do we do with Ben and Carlos first?

validator.description: Student selected 'Subtract twice': confused operation with the structure of combining then comparing

params.description: Third scenario highlights: Did I spend more on only snacks, or on drinks and dessert altogether?

beats.text: Look at the third question. Drinks and dessert need to be put together. That's adding, not subtracting. Then we compare that total to snacks.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: All three scenarios highlight the shared pattern: one thing alone versus two things combined

beats.text: Let me show you the pattern. In every question, one side stands alone and the other side has two things that need combining. You combine first, then you compare the two sides. The answer is combine some things first, then compare.

elements.description: Three real-world scenario questions displayed on screen.

beats.text: What helped you most when figuring out what to do first?
Select what helped most.
options: Thinking about what the question is really asking

validator.description: Student selected option about trigger words

beats.text: Right. Spotting those trigger words is huge. They tell you exactly what to do.

validator.description: Student selected careful reading

beats.text: That's it. Careful reading pays off. The question tells you what it needs.

condition.selected: Thinking about what the question is really asking

validator.description: Student selected mathematical reasoning

beats.text: Exactly. Thinking about what the question REALLY asks. That's mathematical reasoning.

validator.description: Student selected remembering the strategy

beats.text: Yes. Having a strategy ready helps. Find combined total first, then compare works every time.
You learned something important today. You learned to look at a problem before jumping in. You ask: 'What should I find first?'
That's not just math. That's strategic thinking. You're becoming someone who plans before solving.
